title: Computer Vision Concept
subtitle: How Computers See the World Through Pixels!
overview: Discover how computers 'see' images — not as pictures, but as grids of numbers! Explore pixels, RGB colour channels, grayscale, and how a computer reads an image the same way it reads data. Then write real Python code to load, inspect, and manipulate images pixel by pixel — the foundation of all AI vision systems.

keyTopics: Pixel — the smallest unit of a digital image; every image is a grid of pixels
Resolution — the number of pixels (e.g. 1920×1080 = over 2 million pixels!)
RGB Colour Model — each pixel stores 3 values: Red (0–255), Green (0–255), Blue (0–255)
Grayscale — 1 number per pixel (0 = black, 255 = white, in-between = shades of grey)
Image as Array — a computer stores an image as a 2D array (grayscale) or 3D array (colour)
Brightness & Contrast — changing pixel values makes images lighter, darker, or more vivid
Edge Detection — finding places where pixel values change sharply; outlines objects for AI
Real-World Computer Vision — face unlock, self-driving cars, medical imaging, Google Lens, QR codes

codeExamples.title: What a Computer Sees — Image as a Number Grid
codeExamples.description: Create a tiny 5×5 grayscale 'image' by hand as a 2D list. This is exactly how a computer stores any image — just numbers!
codeExamples.code:
# A 5×5 grayscale image — each number is one pixel
# 0 = black,  255 = white,  128 = mid-grey

image = [
    [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0],   # row 0 — all black
    [  0, 255, 255, 255,   0],   # row 1
    [  0, 255, 128, 255,   0],   # row 2 — grey centre
    [  0, 255, 255, 255,   0],   # row 3
    [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0],   # row 4 — all black
]

print("Image stored as numbers:")
for row in image:
    # Print each pixel value padded to 3 digits
    print('  ' + '  '.join(f"{px:3d}" for px in row))

print()
print(f"Image size : {len(image)} rows × {len(image[0])} columns")
print(f"Total pixels: {len(image) * len(image[0])}")
print(f"Top-left pixel value : {image[0][0]} (black)")
print(f"Centre pixel value   : {image[2][2]} (mid-grey)")

codeExamples.title: RGB Pixels — Colour as Three Numbers
codeExamples.description: Every colour pixel is a tuple of (Red, Green, Blue) — each 0 to 255. See how mixing numbers creates any colour.
codeExamples.code:
# Each colour pixel = (Red, Green, Blue) — values 0 to 255

# Define some colours as RGB tuples
colours = {
    "Black"   : (  0,   0,   0),
    "White"   : (255, 255, 255),
    "Red"     : (255,   0,   0),
    "Green"   : (  0, 255,   0),
    "Blue"    : (  0,   0, 255),
    "Yellow"  : (255, 255,   0),   # Red + Green!
    "Cyan"    : (  0, 255, 255),   # Green + Blue!
    "Magenta" : (255,   0, 255),   # Red + Blue!
    "Orange"  : (255, 165,   0),
    "Purple"  : (128,   0, 128),
}

print(f"{'Colour':<10}  R    G    B    Hex Code")
print("-" * 42)
for name, (r, g, b) in colours.items():
    hex_code = f"#{r:02X}{g:02X}{b:02X}"
    print(f"{name:<10}  {r:3d}  {g:3d}  {b:3d}  {hex_code}")

print()
print("Fun fact: #FF0000 is pure red — the same hex")
print("code you see in CSS and web design!")

codeExamples.title: Load and Inspect a Real Image with Pillow
codeExamples.description: Use Python's Pillow library to open an image, check its size, and read individual pixel values — just like a computer vision system does.
codeExamples.code:
# pip install Pillow
from PIL import Image

# ── OPEN AN IMAGE ─────────────────────────────────
# Replace 'photo.jpg' with any image file you have
img = Image.open('photo.jpg')

print("=== Image Info ===")
print(f"Format    : {img.format}")          # JPEG, PNG, etc.
print(f"Mode      : {img.mode}")            # 'RGB', 'L' (grayscale), etc.
print(f"Size      : {img.width} × {img.height} pixels")
print(f"Total px  : {img.width * img.height:,}")

# ── READ PIXEL VALUES ─────────────────────────────
# getpixel((x, y)) — x = column, y = row
top_left  = img.getpixel((0, 0))
center    = img.getpixel((img.width // 2, img.height // 2))

print()
print(f"Top-left pixel  : R={top_left[0]}, G={top_left[1]}, B={top_left[2]}")
print(f"Centre pixel    : R={center[0]},  G={center[1]},  B={center[2]}")

# ── CONVERT TO GRAYSCALE ──────────────────────────
gray = img.convert('L')   # 'L' mode = 1 value per pixel
gray_px = gray.getpixel((img.width // 2, img.height // 2))
print(f"Centre pixel (grayscale): {gray_px}")

# ── SHOW THE IMAGE ────────────────────────────────
img.show()    # Opens the image in your default viewer

codeExamples.title: Manipulate Pixels — Brightness & Invert
codeExamples.description: Change every pixel's value to make an image brighter, darker, or inverted. This is the foundation of all image filters!
codeExamples.code:
from PIL import Image
import os

img  = Image.open('photo.jpg').convert('RGB')
pixels = img.load()   # Pixel access object
width, height = img.size

# ── MAKE IMAGE BRIGHTER ───────────────────────────
bright = Image.new('RGB', (width, height))
bright_px = bright.load()

for y in range(height):
    for x in range(width):
        r, g, b = pixels[x, y]
        # Add 60 to each channel — clamp at 255
        bright_px[x, y] = (
            min(r + 60, 255),
            min(g + 60, 255),
            min(b + 60, 255)
        )

bright.save('photo_bright.jpg')
print("Saved: photo_bright.jpg")

# ── INVERT IMAGE (negative effect) ────────────────
invert = Image.new('RGB', (width, height))
invert_px = invert.load()

for y in range(height):
    for x in range(width):
        r, g, b = pixels[x, y]
        # 255 minus each channel flips it
        invert_px[x, y] = (255 - r, 255 - g, 255 - b)

invert.save('photo_inverted.jpg')
print("Saved: photo_inverted.jpg")

# ── GRAYSCALE CONVERSION FORMULA ──────────────────
# Real grayscale isn't just averaging R+G+B!
# Human eyes are most sensitive to green.
# Standard formula: Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B
gray = Image.new('L', (width, height))
gray_px = gray.load()

for y in range(height):
    for x in range(width):
        r, g, b = pixels[x, y]
        value = int(0.299*r + 0.587*g + 0.114*b)
        gray_px[x, y] = value

gray.save('photo_gray.jpg')
print("Saved: photo_gray.jpg")

codeExamples.title: Edge Detection — Where Pixel Values Change
codeExamples.description: The simplest edge detector: compare each pixel to its neighbour. Big difference = edge! This is how AI finds outlines in images.
codeExamples.code:
from PIL import Image, ImageFilter

img  = Image.open('photo.jpg').convert('L')  # grayscale

# ── METHOD 1: Pillow built-in edge filter ─────────
edge_img = img.filter(ImageFilter.FIND_EDGES)
edge_img.save('photo_edges.jpg')
print("Saved: photo_edges.jpg")

# ── METHOD 2: Manual — compare neighbours ─────────
# If a pixel is much brighter than its right neighbour
# → that's an edge!
pixels = img.load()
width, height = img.size
manual = Image.new('L', (width, height), 0)
out_px = manual.load()

for y in range(height):
    for x in range(width - 1):   # stop 1 before edge
        current  = pixels[x,     y]
        next_px  = pixels[x + 1, y]
        diff = abs(int(current) - int(next_px))
        # Amplify the difference so edges are visible
        out_px[x, y] = min(diff * 5, 255)

manual.save('photo_edges_manual.jpg')
print("Saved: photo_edges_manual.jpg")

print()
print("Edge detection finds outlines by looking for")
print("places where brightness changes sharply.")
print("This is how AI systems detect object borders!")

activity.title: Pixel Art Grid + Python Image Inspector
parts: Laptop with Python + Pillow installed (pip install Pillow)
Any JPG or PNG image file
steps: Pixel Art Round: On grid paper, design a simple 8×8 image (smiley face, heart, letter). Shade each square — this is your pixel grid!
Encode it: number each row and column 0–7. Write the brightness value (0=black, 128=grey, 255=white) for each square into a 2D table.
Run Code Example 1 — create your own 5×5 number grid in Python. Try changing values and predict what the image 'looks like'.
Run Code Example 2 — explore RGB colour mixing. Challenge: find the RGB values for your favourite colour!
Run Code Example 3 with a real photo — print its width, height, and read pixel values from different corners.
Run Code Example 4 — save brightened and inverted versions of your photo. Compare them side by side.
Bonus Challenge: Run Code Example 5 — edge detection on your photo. Can you see where the computer found the outlines?

options: A type of camera lens
The smallest individual unit of a digital image — a tiny square of colour
A Python library for images
The file size of an image
quiz.explanation: Pixel = Picture Element. Every digital image is made of a grid of pixels — zoom in far enough on any photo and you'll see the individual coloured squares. A computer stores each pixel as one or more numbers.

quiz.q: How does a computer store a COLOUR pixel?
options: As a single number from 0 to 100
As the name of the colour (e.g. 'red')
As three numbers — Red, Green, Blue — each from 0 to 255
As a binary ON/OFF value
quiz.explanation: Every colour pixel is an RGB triplet: (R, G, B). Each channel ranges from 0 (none) to 255 (full). Red=(255,0,0), Green=(0,255,0), Blue=(0,0,255). Mix them to make any colour!

quiz.q: What does a pixel value of 0 mean in GRAYSCALE?
options: White
Transparent
Black
The pixel is missing
quiz.explanation: In grayscale, 0 = pure black and 255 = pure white. Values in between are shades of grey. A grayscale image stores just ONE number per pixel instead of three (RGB).

quiz.q: What is the RGB value for PURE WHITE?
quiz.explanation: White = maximum of all three channels: (255, 255, 255). Black = minimum of all three: (0, 0, 0). Grey = equal values in the middle, like (128, 128, 128).

options: The brightness of the image
The number of colours used
The total number of pixels — width × height (e.g. 1920×1080)
The file format (JPG or PNG)
quiz.explanation: Resolution = how many pixels the image contains. A 1920×1080 image has 1,920 × 1,080 = over 2 million pixels! More pixels = more detail = bigger file size.

quiz.q: How does a computer store a colour image as data?
options: As a painting saved in a special art format
As a 3D array of numbers — rows × columns × 3 colour channels
As a list of shape names (circle, square, etc.)
As compressed audio data
quiz.explanation: A colour image is a 3D array: height × width × 3. The 3 represents R, G, B channels. A 100×100 colour image stores 100 × 100 × 3 = 30,000 numbers. A grayscale image is 2D: height × width.

quiz.q: In Python Pillow, what does img.getpixel((x, y)) return for a colour image?
options: A single brightness value
The pixel's filename
A tuple of (Red, Green, Blue) values for that pixel
The image's total file size
quiz.explanation: getpixel((x, y)) returns the pixel at column x, row y as an (R, G, B) tuple. For example, (255, 0, 0) means pure red. You can use this to inspect exactly what colour is at any location in an image.

quiz.q: What does converting an image to GRAYSCALE do?
options: Removes all pixels below a certain brightness
Reduces each pixel from 3 colour values (RGB) to 1 brightness value
Makes the image smaller in file size only
Converts the image to black and white pixels with no grey
quiz.explanation: Grayscale conversion collapses R, G, B into a single brightness value per pixel using a weighted formula: Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B. Green is weighted most because human eyes are most sensitive to it!

quiz.q: What does EDGE DETECTION do to an image?
options: Removes the border around the image
Makes all colours brighter
Finds places where pixel values change sharply — highlighting outlines and boundaries
Compresses the image to a smaller file
quiz.explanation: Edges appear where pixel brightness changes suddenly — like the border between a face and the background. Edge detection highlights these changes, giving AI a way to find objects and shapes in a scene.

quiz.q: What is the RGB value for YELLOW?
quiz.explanation: Yellow = Red + Green at full intensity: (255, 255, 0). Think of it like mixing paint — red and green light combine to make yellow. Cyan = (0,255,255), Magenta = (255,0,255).

quiz.q: In our brightness code, why do we use min(r + 60, 255)?
options: To make sure the value never goes below 0
To make sure the brightness value never exceeds 255 — the maximum for a colour channel
To speed up the calculation
To convert the value to grayscale
quiz.explanation: RGB values must stay within 0–255. If a pixel has R=220 and we add 60, we get 280 — which is invalid! min(280, 255) clamps it to 255. Without this clamp, the image data would be corrupted.

quiz.q: What does INVERTING an image do to each pixel?
options: Rotates the pixel 180 degrees
Sets every channel to 0
Subtracts each channel value from 255, creating a photographic negative effect
Swaps the red and blue channels
quiz.explanation: Invert = 255 minus each channel. A bright pixel (200,200,200) becomes dark (55,55,55). A red pixel (255,0,0) becomes cyan (0,255,255). This creates a photographic negative — like the old film negatives!

quiz.q: What Python library do we use to open and manipulate images in this session?
options: numpy
scikit-learn
Pillow (PIL)
matplotlib
quiz.explanation: Pillow (imported as PIL) is Python's most popular image library. It can open, resize, crop, filter, and save images in dozens of formats. Install it with: pip install Pillow

quiz.q: Which real-world technology uses Computer Vision?
options: FM radio broadcasting
RFID card reading
Face unlock on smartphones and self-driving car cameras
Bluetooth speaker pairing
quiz.explanation: Computer Vision powers face unlock (phone recognises your face from a camera image), self-driving cars (detecting roads, signs, pedestrians), medical imaging (AI reading X-rays), Google Lens, and QR code scanning!

quiz.q: A 640×480 pixel image contains how many pixels in total?
quiz.explanation: 640 × 480 = 307,200 pixels. A colour version of this image stores 307,200 × 3 = 921,600 individual numbers (one R, G, B value per pixel)! That's nearly 1 million numbers for a small image.

quiz.q: Why is the GREEN channel weighted most heavily (0.587) in the grayscale formula?
options: Green pixels are the largest on a screen
Green is the most common colour in photographs
Human eyes are most sensitive to green light — it affects perceived brightness the most
It is a random weighting chosen by programmers
quiz.explanation: The human eye has more green-sensitive cone cells than red or blue. So green contributes most to how bright we perceive something. The formula Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B is based on human vision science — not just maths!
